network.overall_strategy: This network targets threonine through a chemically tighter beta-hydroxy-alpha-amino acid logic rather than an unsupported direct late-stage installation. Hydrothermal chemistry generates reduced C1/C2/C3 feedstocks and alpha-keto acid hubs (formate, acetate, pyruvate). Surface chemistry supplies glycolaldehyde, acetaldehyde, HCN, aminonitriles, and sugar-derived carbonyl partners under mineral-mediated wet-dry and photochemical regimes. Biochemical/prebiotic assembly then uses two convergent target-forming motifs: (i) aldol-type addition of formaldehyde to glycine-derived imine/aminonitrile equivalents followed by hydrolysis to serine-like beta-hydroxy amino acids and C-methyl extension using acetaldehyde-derived amino-carbonyl chemistry; and (ii) alpha-ketobutyrate/2-amino-3-oxobutanoate style intermediates reduced to threonine. The network is intentionally redundant around hub molecules HCN, glycolaldehyde, pyruvate, acetaldehyde, and aminoacetonitrile, with 10 pathway variants reaching threonine through different environmental entries while avoiding unsupported direct conversion of vague precursor pools to target.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed reduction of CO2 to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 to surface-bound formyl/formate equivalents that desorb as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Fe-S hydrothermal minerals are among the best-supported catalysts for abiotic CO2 reduction to formate under vent conditions, providing the foundational C1 feedstock for downstream carbonyl chemistry (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Barge et al., 2019; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal carbon fixation to acetate
reactions.mechanism: Sequential reduction and C-C bond formation from C1 units on Fe-S surfaces yields acetyl-level products released as acetate.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate formation from CO2/H2 under hydrothermal mineral catalysis is consistent with iron-sulfur world and vent geochemistry and provides a realistic C2 branch connected to pyruvate and aldehyde production (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Muchowska et al., 2019; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Magnetite-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of pyruvate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive carboxylation/C-C coupling transforms acetate-level carbon into alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate and related carboxylic acids can arise in hydrothermal analog networks from CO2-derived reduced carbon on iron-bearing minerals, making pyruvate a justified central metabolic hub (Muchowska et al., 2019; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid decarboxylation gives the corresponding aldehyde, here acetaldehyde, with loss of CO2.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate decarboxylation to acetaldehyde is chemically straightforward and much more defensible than prior vague endgame chemistry; it creates a realistic aldehyde entry into Strecker and aldol-type networks relevant to threonine precursors (Copley et al., 2007; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface photoredox generation of formaldehyde from formate
reactions.mechanism: Photoredox conversion of formate/formic acid on semiconductor mineral surfaces yields reduced carbonyl species including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Although less direct than vent reduction, surface photoredox transformation of formate into more reduced carbonyl chemistry is a plausible connecting step between hydrothermal C1 output and surface sugar chemistry; included as a moderate-confidence bridge rather than an unsupported HCN-from-formaldehyde shortcut (Cleaves, 2008; Patel et al., 2015, contextual connection).

reactions.name: Borate-stabilized formation of glycolaldehyde from formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-supported C-C coupling/formose initiation converts formaldehyde into C2 sugar aldehydes, with borate suppressing uncontrolled tar formation.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolaldehyde is a well-motivated sugar precursor and hub for prebiotic carbonyl chemistry; borate-stabilized formose-like processes improve plausibility relative to free-solution formaldehyde networks (Ricardo et al., 2004; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic generation and concentration of HCN

conditions.other: UV photochemistry, sulfur-rich geothermal field, anoxic atmosphere contribution

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical nitrogen-carbon coupling in reduced sulfur-rich settings generates cyanide inventory that accumulates upon drying and rewetting.
reactions.reasoning: This step is included only as a network connection to a surface cyanide reservoir and is framed conservatively: HCN availability on early Earth likely involved atmospheric and photochemical contributions rather than a single neat flask reaction. Cyanosulfidic scenarios support persistent cyanide chemistry in UV-irradiated surface settings (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile from formaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN
reactions.mechanism: Imine formation from formaldehyde and ammonia followed by cyanide addition yields the aminonitrile aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Explicit aminonitrile formation is used here to avoid the prior error of collapsing Strecker chemistry directly into amino acids. Aminoacetonitrile is a credible glycine precursor under prebiotic conditions (Miller, 1957; Danger et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine

conditions.other: geothermal cycling, neutral to mildly acidic hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Sequential hydration and nitrile hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile into the corresponding amino acid glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Separating nitrile hydrolysis from Strecker formation provides a more chemically defensible route to glycine and creates a clear amino donor/carbon framework for later threonine-forming chemistry (Danger et al., 2012; Peltzer et al., 1984).

reactions.name: Aminoacetonitrile-formaldehyde addition to serinal precursor

conditions.other: concentrated pool, reversible imine formation and hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: The glycine-nitrile equivalent adds to formaldehyde through imine/carbanion chemistry to yield a beta-hydroxylated alpha-amino aldehyde precursor analogous to serinal after partial hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Using aminoacetonitrile as a glycine equivalent for beta-hydroxylation avoids an implausible direct construction of threonine from undefined pools. This step is somewhat speculative but is chemically more focused and mirrors known aldehyde additions of aminonitrile/imino nitrile species in Strecker-related chemistry (Eschenmoser, 2007; Danger et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Oxidation-hydrolysis of serinal to serine

conditions.other: hydration/oxidation at mineral-water interfaces under anoxic ferric/photochemical microcycles

reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde oxidation and nitrile-derived hydrolysis produce the stable beta-hydroxy amino acid serine.
reactions.reasoning: Serine is a credible prebiotic branch product from glycine-equivalent plus formaldehyde chemistry and serves as a chemically sharper beta-hydroxy scaffold than prior unsupported direct threonine-forming proposals. Some oxidation chemistry remains context-dependent, so this step is moderately speculative but structurally coherent (Kitadai et al., 2018; Danger et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Acetaldehyde-mediated C-methyl extension of serine to alpha-amino-beta-ketobutyrate

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling, imine/enolizable carbonyl chemistry in concentrated pools

reactions.mechanism: Condensation of the serine-derived amino aldehyde/enolizable equivalent with acetaldehyde generates a beta-keto-alpha-amino acid framework represented as alpha-amino-beta-ketobutyrate.
reactions.reasoning: This is the key target-directed repair relative to previous failed attempts: threonine is approached through a beta-keto precursor whose reduction gives the target stereochemical class, rather than by an unsupported direct transformation from vague pools. The exact prebiotic micro-mechanism is speculative, but beta-keto-to-beta-hydroxy amino acid logic is far more chemically defensible than earlier endgames (Eschenmoser, 2007; Weber, 1981 for carbonyl-amino acid condensations in prebiotic settings).

reactions.name: Reduction of alpha-amino-beta-ketobutyrate to threonine

conditions.other: redox-active mineral interface or dissolved Fe2+, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Reduction of the beta-keto group in alpha-amino-beta-ketobutyrate yields the beta-hydroxy amino acid threonine.
reactions.reasoning: This final transformation is chemically direct and substantially more plausible than converting 2-aminobutyrate into threonine. Reduction of a beta-keto precursor is the correct functional-group logic for forming threonine as a beta-hydroxy-alpha-amino acid; while specific prebiotic reductants remain an open question, H2/Fe2+-mediated mineral-interface reductions are reasonable (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kitadai & Maruyama, 2018).

pathways.name: Canonical hydrothermal-to-surface aminonitrile route
pathways.description: CO2 reduction to formate, photoredox entry to formaldehyde, Strecker formation of aminoacetonitrile, formaldehyde addition to serinal, then serine, beta-keto precursor, and threonine.

pathways.name: Acetate-pyruvate-acetaldehyde assisted route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetate and pyruvate production feed acetaldehyde generation; surface cyanide and glycine-nitrile chemistry then converge to threonine through the beta-keto intermediate.

pathways.name: Sugar-first route via glycolaldehyde-fed formaldehyde pool
pathways.description: Hydrothermal C1 carbon enters borate-stabilized sugar chemistry first, then equilibrates back into carbonyl feedstock supporting HCN and aminoacetonitrile formation before target assembly.

pathways.name: Glycine-through-hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile is first hydrolyzed to glycine, representing a pool-transfer route where glycine and its nitrile equivalent coexist before re-entry into beta-hydroxylation chemistry.

pathways.name: Pyruvate-dominant route
pathways.description: Strong hydrothermal pyruvate production supplies acetaldehyde directly, while surface carbonyl and cyanide chemistry independently supplies the aminoacetonitrile branch.

pathways.name: Dual-carbonyl convergence route
pathways.description: Formaldehyde-derived aminoacetonitrile and hydrothermal acetaldehyde are generated in parallel and only meet late at the beta-keto precursor stage.

pathways.name: Formate-centered redundant route
pathways.description: Formate acts as the principal hydrothermal hub, feeding both acetate/pyruvate/acetaldehyde and surface formaldehyde/glycolaldehyde branches before convergence on threonine.

pathways.name: Aminoacetonitrile-reservoir route
pathways.description: Surface synthesis accumulates aminoacetonitrile as a reservoir species that either hydrolyzes to glycine or reacts onward with formaldehyde to beta-hydroxylated intermediates leading to threonine.

pathways.name: Glycolaldehyde-supported carbonyl diversification route
pathways.description: Surface borate chemistry broadens carbonyl availability, stabilizing the aldehyde network that supports cyanide capture and later beta-hydroxy amino acid construction.

pathways.name: Full cross-environment integrated route
pathways.description: The densest route uses hydrothermal reduction, surface photochemistry, sugar chemistry, Strecker aminonitrile chemistry, and late prebiotic carbonyl reduction to reach threonine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The early hydrothermal-to-surface flow is topologically coherent, and aminoacetonitrile formation from formaldehyde/NH3/HCN is plausible. However, the pathway depends on weakly supported steps: formate to formaldehyde as a surface photoredox route is poorly justified, formaldehyde plus ammonia to HCN is chemically unsound as written, and the late biochemical conversion from serine plus acetaldehyde to alpha-amino-beta-ketobutyrate lacks a clear prebiotic mechanism. Target formation is conceivable only if several speculative carbonyl-amination steps are granted.
suggestions: Replace rxn_007 with a more realistic exogenous or geochemical source of HCN/formamide/cyanide feedstock
Recast rxn_005 as formaldehyde generation from methanol or photochemical CO/CO2 reduction with explicit mineral/UV chemistry
Introduce a more plausible route to beta-hydroxy amino acid precursors, such as cyanohydrin/aminonitrile chemistry from acetaldehyde and glyoxylate-like partners
Clarify redox chemistry for rxn_011 and rxn_013 with explicit reductants/catalysts

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the more coherent pathways because pyruvate decarboxylation to acetaldehyde is chemically credible and creates a useful C2 branch feeding the target logic. Nevertheless, the pathway still inherits major weaknesses from the HCN-generation step and from the unsupported serine-to-alpha-amino-beta-ketobutyrate transformation. The endgame remains the main bottleneck.
suggestions: Use acetaldehyde directly in Strecker-type chemistry to access threonine-related aminonitriles instead of going through serine
Define a plausible aldol/transamination sequence to alpha-ketobutyrate or aminoketobutyrate
Add explicit minerals/conditions for redox management in the biochemical stage

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The sugar-first framing is plausible at the level of broad prebiotic carbonyl chemistry, but the specific formaldehyde-to-glycolaldehyde step is underdescribed and usually requires stronger justification than simple borate stabilization. More importantly, glycolaldehyde is not convincingly used to improve the target route, so this pathway reads as topological redundancy rather than mechanistic convergence.
suggestions: Make glycolaldehyde an actual precursor to a target-relevant intermediate rather than a side hub
Specify aldol or cyanohydrin chemistry connecting glycolaldehyde to threonine-class precursors
Strengthen the origin of formaldehyde and cyanide under surface conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine is one of the stronger features of the network. But glycine is then not cleanly leveraged into a validated threonine-forming sequence. The path from glycine/aminoacetonitrile chemistry to serinal/serine and onward to the aminoketone target precursor remains too speculative.
suggestions: Introduce glycine-dependent Mannich or Strecker chemistry with acetaldehyde to form threonine-related aminonitriles more directly
Avoid requiring serinal as a discrete intermediate unless its generation is well justified

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pyruvate-dominant variant benefits from plausible hydrothermal chemistry and a credible bridge to acetaldehyde. It is still undermined by the noncredible cyanide-generation step and the poorly established late-stage beta-hydroxy amino acid elaboration. Among the set, this is relatively strong upstream but weak downstream.
suggestions: Branch from pyruvate into alpha-ketobutyrate chemistry explicitly, rather than via serine
Use known prebiotic keto-acid amination/reduction motifs for the final two steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Dual-carbonyl convergence is conceptually attractive, especially using formaldehyde and acetaldehyde as distinct C1/C2 inputs. Still, the actual transformations linking them to the aminoketobutyrate node are not chemically well grounded. This is a promising design idea with insufficient mechanistic support.
suggestions: Specify whether the key C-C bond formation is an aldol, Mannich, Strecker-variant, or cyanohydrin pathway
Introduce a validated carbonyl precursor such as aminoacetaldehyde or glyoxylate if needed to support beta-hydroxylation logic

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route demonstrates good network redundancy around formate and its descendants, which is a topological strength. However, formate-centered redundancy does not overcome the chemistry problem: the key surface conversion to formaldehyde and the final prebiotic threonine-forming steps remain poorly supported.
suggestions: Keep formate as an energy/carbon reservoir but use literature-supported downstream carbonyl chemistry
Add explicit concentration and wet-dry conditions for surface-stage carbonyl buildup

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Treating aminoacetonitrile as a reservoir intermediate is sensible and chemically motivated. But the pathway still relies on an implausible cyanide source and a speculative elaboration of aminoacetonitrile into the target skeleton. The reservoir concept is stronger than the target conversion chemistry.
suggestions: Leverage aminoacetonitrile in better-established aminonitrile exchange or aldehyde addition chemistry
Consider threonine aminonitrile as a more direct branchpoint if the network is revised

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The carbonyl diversification idea is conceptually useful, but this pathway suffers from weak integration of glycolaldehyde into the final synthetic logic. It appears more as a side product than a true convergence point for threonine synthesis.
suggestions: Connect glycolaldehyde directly to amino alcohol or alpha-keto precursor chemistry
Reduce reliance on formaldehyde-only logic for building the beta-hydroxy amino acid skeleton

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best-integrated route in terms of network architecture and environmental flow: hydrothermal C1/C2/C3 chemistry feeds surface aminonitrile chemistry and then a biochemical assembly stage. The main weakness is not topology but chemistry. Several key transformations in the late stage are speculative, and one early surface cyanide-forming step is not credible as written.
suggestions: Retain the hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical architecture but redesign the threonine endgame
Use a direct Strecker route from a beta-hydroxy aldehyde or a keto-acid amination/reduction route to threonine
Explicitly specify mineral agents for each surface reaction and mechanistic details for the biochemical reductions

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is strong in topology and environmental integration but only moderate to weak in chemical feasibility. Positives include sufficient node count, a connected graph, multiple hubs, real cross-environment flow, and several plausible upstream hydrothermal steps such as CO2 to formate, acetate, pyruvate, and pyruvate decarboxylation to acetaldehyde. Aminoacetonitrile formation and hydrolysis to glycine are also reasonable. Major weaknesses are concentrated in several key reactions: the proposed surface generation of formaldehyde from formate is not well established; the conversion of formaldehyde plus ammonia into HCN is chemically implausible; and the late-stage route from aminoacetonitrile/serine chemistry to alpha-amino-beta-ketobutyrate and threonine lacks convincing prebiotic precedent or mechanistic detail. The network does produce the target in graph terms, but the final synthetic logic is too speculative for approval. An improved version should preserve the current hub structure while replacing the target-forming module with a more direct and literature-grounded aminonitrile or alpha-keto acid route.
